:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 633 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 30% of the prison population.



``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Release Eligibility Determined?

Most felony offenders receive indeterminate sentences that contain a minimum and a maximum term. The maximum term may not be greater than the maximum provided by law, and the minimum term may not be less than the minimum provided by law and may not be more than 90% of the maximum term.10 Offenders with indeterminate sentences are eligible for parole when the minimum term imposed by the court, minus good time earned, is complete. When an inmate begins imprisonment, the Department of Corrections calculates a projected parole eligibility date based on the full potential good time allowance and the minimum sentence. This calculation is not “an award of or entitlement to good time but is merely for administrative purposes.” Inmates who have committed an escape, attempted escape, or assisted others to escape from any institution during a sentence are ineligible for parole on that sentence. Inmates who have committed assault with a deadly weapon on another inmate, an officer, or a staff member during a sentence are also ineligible for parole during that sentence.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people constituted the most number of people eligible for parole but still in prison for a new crime, accounting for 77% (487 people) of the parole-eligible prison population.


``` ::: ::: ### Gender ### Age ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Most Serious Sentenced Offense

In 2020, there were 237 people who were parole eligible but still in prison for non-violent offenses, accounting for 37% of the parole-eligible prison population in prison for new crimes.

``` ::: :::

## Disparities
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Descriptive Statistics

``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
Race, ethincity, age, and gender

``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Relative Rate Index

The relative rate index (RRI) provides a measure of disparity in outcomes for different racial and ethnic groups by comparing the rates at which individuals of different races and ethnic groups experience incarceration. The rate for each specific racial and ethnic group is computed by dividing the population of interest within that group by the total state population for that particular group.

To calculate the RRI, the rate of each outcome of interest for individuals who are Black non-Hispanic and Hispanic any race, is divided by the rate at which the outcome occurs for individuals who are White. A RRI of 1 indicates no disparity. RRIs greater than 1 indicate the outcome occurs more frequently for individuals who are Black or Hispanic, and an RRI less than 1 indicates an outcome occurs less frequently than for individuals who are White.
